prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
“Deep-work marathons” – the cult of blocking off four, six, or even eight uninterrupted hours every day to do cognitively demanding tasks.

Why it’s overrated:
1. Most knowledge jobs are interrupt-driven systems. Your code build fails, the client’s lawyer finally replies, a key team-mate is leaving in two weeks—if you “go dark” for half the day you simply offload the friction to someone else (or to your future self after 6 p.m.).  
2. Cognitive stamina is real. For most people the quality curve for genuinely deep thinking is essentially flat after ~90–120 min; the extra hours become quasi-deep make-work that feels virtuous but produces marginal insight.  
3. It collapses the difference between availability and responsiveness. You can be highly focused yet still check Slack twice an hour; the danger is context switching, not the tab being open. Teaching people to disappear teaches them to manage their calendar instead of their attention.  
4. It ignores task heterogeneity. Some problems need four focused hours, others need four five-minute bursts sprinkled through a day of meetings. Turning the schedule into an all-or-nothing shrine converts a tactic into a dogma.

Deep work is a powerful tool; turning it into a daily six-hour escape pod is an over-correction that solves the wrong problem—making knowledge work look like 19th-century factory labor instead of learning to toggle intelligently between focus and necessary coordination.
